revision.text:
[[en:Daibutsuyō]][[es:Daibutsuyō]][[fr:Daibutsuyō]][[ja:大仏様]][[zh:大佛样]] {{Short description|Japanese religious architectural style|qq=}} {{italic title|qq=}} [[File:TodaijiNandaimon0185.jpg|275 px|[[Tōdai-ji]]'s Nandaimon is one of the few extant examples of the ''daibutsuyō''|thumb]]{{nihongo|'''''Daibutsuyō'''''|大仏様|lit. great Buddha style|qq=}} is a Japanese religious {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} which emerged in the late 12th or early 13th century. Together with ''{{ill|Wayō|en|Wayō|qid=Q3566879|lt=Wayō|1=Wayō}}'' and ''{{ill|Zenshūyō|en|Zenshūyō|qid=Q3575224|lt=Zenshūyō|1=Zenshūyō}}'', it is one of the three most significant styles developed by Japanese Buddhism on the basis of Chinese models. Originally called {{nihongo|''tenjikuyō''|天竺様|lit. Indian style|qq=}}, because it had nothing to do with India it was rechristened by scholar Ōta Hirotarō during the 20th century, and the new term stuck.{{Jaanus2|d/daibutsuyou|Daibutsuyou|4 June 2011|qq=}} Ōta derived the name from Chōgen's work, particularly [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Daibutsuden. Soon abandoned after its creator's death, probably because it didn't harmonize with Japanese tastes, it nonetheless influenced other building styles with its rational solutions.Nishi, Hozumi (1996:20-21) The combination of ''wayō'' and ''daibutsuyō'' in particular became so frequent that sometimes it is classed separately by scholars under the name {{nihongo|''[[:en:Shin-wayō|Shin-wayō]]''|新和様|new ''wayō''|qq=}}.{{Harvnb|Fletcher|Cruickshank|1996|qq=}}, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=Gt1jTpXAThwC&pg=PA738 738] This grandiose and monumental style is the antithesis of the simple and traditional ''Wayō'' style. The Nandaimon at [[Tōdai-ji]] and the [[Dō (architecture)|Amida-dō]] at {{ill|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|en|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|qid=Q3077393|lt=Jōdo-ji|1=Jōdo-ji (Ono)}} in Ono are its best extant examples.{{Harvnb|Fletcher|Cruickshank|1996|qq=}}, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=Gt1jTpXAThwC&pg=PA737 737] ==History== The style was introduced by priest {{ill|Chōgen (monk)|en|Chōgen (monk)|qid=Q679462|lt=Chōgen|1=Chōgen (monk)}}, who in 1180 directed the reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]], which had been destroyed during the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|qid=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}}. Chōgen had just come back from the last of his three travels to China and therefore chose as a basis for the work {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} architecture. He was supported in his innovative work by first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. Of his work at the temple only three structures remain, the already mentioned Nandaimon, which remains the best Daibutsuyō example, the [[Dō (architecture)|Kaizandō]] and the [[Dō (architecture)|Hokkedō]]. The gate's most characteristic features are the six-tier bracket groups (''[[:en:Tokyō|Tokyō]]'') projecting directly out of the columns and connected to each other by ties as long as the facade. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} the temple's {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, the Daibutsuden, was also rebuilt in the style, to which it would give its name. Chōgen built other buildings in this style near and around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, of which the [[Dō (architecture)|Amida-dō]] at {{ill|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|en|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|qid=Q3077393|lt=Jōdo-ji|1=Jōdo-ji (Ono)}} in Ono is a good extant example. The style declined quickly after its creator's death, probably because it did not agree with Japanese tastes. Structural elements are treated as design elements, and the building's deliberate roughness is supposed to be part of its beauty, but the concept was probably too alien to Chōgen's contemporaries, and was rejected.{{cite web|last=Kudō|first=Yoshiaki|title=Daibutsuyō|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%8F%E6%A7%98%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130218173440/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%8F%E6%A7%98%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/|url-status=dead|archive-date=18 February 2013|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|access-date=6 April 2011|qq=}} ==Features== [[File:Mutesaki tokyou.jpg|thumb|Nandaimon (detail). Note the exposed ''tōrihijiki'']] The Daibutsuyō style was short-lived but innovative, and many of the ideas it introduced were adopted by other styles as well. In particular, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} the traditional {{ill|Wayō|en|Wayō|qid=Q3566879|lt=Wayō|1=Wayō}} style was so heavily influenced that the mix of the two is sometimes called [[:en:Shin-wayō|Shin-wayō]]. *Thick [[:en:Woodwork|Woodwork]] and imposing general look *Use of penetrating tie beams During the Heian period temples were built using only non-penetrating tie beams ({{nihongo|''nageshi''|長押|qq=}}) made to fit around columns and pillars and nailed. The ''daibutsuyō'' style, first, and the ''{{ill|Zenshūyō|en|Zenshūyō|qid=Q3575224|lt=Zenshūyō|1=Zenshūyō}}'' style, later, replaced them with penetrating tie-beams ({{nihongo|''nuki''|貫|qq=}}), which actually pierced the column, and were therefore much more effective against earthquakes.{{cite book|last=Hamashima|first=Masashi|title=Jisha Kenchiku no Kanshō Kiso Chishiki|year=1999|publisher=Shibundō|location=Tokyo|pages=160|language=ja|qq=}}Nishi, Hozumi (1996:24-25) The ''nageshi'' was however retained as a purely decorative element.{{Jaanus2|n/nageshi|Nageshi|6 April 2011|qq=}} *Thick, visible structural elements with decorative function As already mentioned, many structural elements are left uncovered and have a decorative function. For example, the roof's supporting members are not covered by a ceiling and are therefore fully visible from within the temple. The Nandaimon's stabilizing bracket ties ({{nihongo|tōrihijiki|通り肘木|qq=}}){{Jaanus2|t/tooshihijiki|Tooshihijiki|18 April 2011|qq=}} which run the entire width of the gate are also fully visible (see photo on the right). (Other styles hide them, at least partially.) Structural elements are much thicker than in Zen buildings. *''Sashihijiki'' The {{nihongo|''sashihijiki''|挿肘木|qq=}} is a bracket arm inserted directly into a pillar instead of resting onto a supporting block on top of a pillar, as was normal in the preceding ''wayō'' style (see photo on the right). At Tōdai-ji, both the Nandaimon and the Daibutsuden have six ''sashihijiki'' one on top of the other (''mutesaki tokyō''). (On the subject, see also the article [[:en:Tokyō|Tokyō]]). *''Ōgidaruki'' Another detail unique to this style are the {{nihongo|''ōgidaruki''|扇垂木|lit. fan rafters|qq=}}. The rafters supporting each roof corner spread from a single point, in a fan-like pattern. * ''Kibana'' The tips of each protruding beam ends in a nose-like structure called {{nihongo|''kibana''|木鼻|lit. wooden nose|qq=}}. ==Examples of the Daibutsuyō style== File:Nandaimon.jpg|
Tōdai-ji's Nandaimon
File:Toudai-ji kaizandou.jpg|
Tōdai-ji's Kaizan-dō
File:Todaiji hokkedo.jpg|
Tōdai-ji's Hokke-dō
File:Jodoji Ono Hyōgo01n3200.jpg|
Jōdo-ji's Amida-dō
 ==See also== *[[Japanese Buddhist architecture#Heian period|Japanese Buddhist architecture - Heian period]] *''[[Setchūyō]]'' ==Notes== {{Reflist|30em|qq=}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book |last1= Fletcher |first1= Sir Banister |author-link=Banister Fletcher |last2=Cruickshank |first2=Dan |title= Sir Banister Fletcher's a history of architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Gt1jTpXAThwC&pg=PT1 |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=1996 |orig-year=1896 |edition=20th illustrated |publisher=Architectural Press |isbn=0-7506-2267-9 }} *{{cite web |title=JAANUS |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ |publisher=[[Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]] |ref=CITEREFJAANUS }} *{{cite book |last1= Nishi |first1= Kazuo |last2=Hozumi |first2=Kazuo |title=What is Japanese architecture? |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oZl_yEJGtUYC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=1996 |orig-year=1983 |edition=illustrated |publisher=Kodansha International |isbn=4-7700-1992-0 }} *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko Kimura |last3=Yew |first3=Tan Hong |title= Introduction to Japanese architecture |series=Periplus Asian architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVcsvjK6ZswC |access-date=2010-01-11 |year=2004 |edition=illustrated |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-7946-0100-6 }} {{commons Category|Daibutsuyō|qq=}} {{Japanese architectural elements|qq=}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan|qq=}} [[en:Daibutsuyō]] {{Commons category|Daibutsuyō}} [[es:Daibutsuyō]] [[fr:Daibutsuyō]] [[ja:大仏様]] [[zh:大佛样]] {{wikidata link|Q3012037}} [[Category:Japanese architectural history]] [[Category:Japanese architectural styles]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pre-modern Sino-Japanese culture]] [[Category:寺院建築]]
